title: Chinese Tomato and Egg Stir-Fry

subs.name: canned whole tomatoes, drained
subs.notes: use in the same way; reduce sugar slightly as canned tomatoes can be sweeter

ingredients.name: spring onions, sliced (white and green separated)
sugar
visual_checks: balances the tomato acidity; adjust to taste
ingredients.notes: balances the tomato acidity; adjust to taste

subs.name: a pinch of salt and a dash of vinegar
subs.notes: soy sauce adds depth — worth seeking out

subs.name: skip entirely or add a tiny amount of mustard oil off heat

steps.text: Beat the eggs with a large pinch of salt until the yolks and whites are fully combined. The eggs should flow freely off the fork.

Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok or kadai over medium-high heat until shimmering. Pour in the eggs. Leave for 10 seconds until the edges puff up, then scramble gently with a spatula, moving the curds around until just set but still glossy. Remove the eggs to a plate — they should be underdone at this stage.
Add the remaining oil to the wok. Add the white parts of the spring onion and garlic. Stir on medium heat for 30 seconds.
Add the tomato wedges, soy sauce and sugar. Toss and cook on medium-high heat for 3–4 minutes until the tomatoes soften and release their juices, forming a loose sauce. Taste and add salt if needed.
Return the eggs to the wok. Fold everything together gently for 1 minute — the eggs will finish cooking in the tomato juices. Remove from heat.
Drizzle with sesame oil if using, scatter over the green parts of the spring onion and serve immediately over steamed rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tomato → canned whole tomatoes, drained (use in the same way; reduce sugar slightly as canned tomatoes can be sweeter); soy-sauce → a pinch of salt and a dash of vinegar (soy sauce adds depth — worth seeking out); gingelly-oil → skip entirely or add a tiny amount of mustard oil off heat
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is one of the easiest Chinese dishes to make in an Indian kitchen. All ingredients are everyday items — thakkali (tomatoes), muttai (eggs), poondu (garlic) and oil. Use a kadai on medium-high heat. Gingelly oil (nallennai) doubles as sesame oil. Soy sauce is optional but widely available; a dash of it deepens the sauce significantly.
